seo.meta_title: Logto vs Auth.js for Next.js App Router (2026): Which Auth Tool Wins? (Updated Week 24, 2026)
seo.meta_description: Logto vs Auth.js: which one actually works better with Next.js App Router? I break down setup time (20 minutes vs 20 minutes), learning curves, and what you'll pay as you grow.
seo.og_title: Logto or Auth.js: What Actually Matters for Next.js
seo.og_description: I tested Logto and Auth.js with Next.js App Router. Here's my honest comparison of pricing, DX, and which one I'd actually ship.

hero.headline: Logto vs Auth.js for Next.js Authentication
hero.subheadline: An honest comparison of Logto and Auth.js — pricing, DX, setup time, and which one we recommend for Next.js App Router projects in 2026.

quick_summary: Logto vs Auth.js is a common comparison we hear from Next.js developers. Logto (4.1★) and Auth.js (4.6★) both have loyal followings. Our analysis shows Auth.js has the edge for most Next.js projects due to its 8/10 DX score. Logto is a strong alternative if yes, up to 5,000 mau better matches your budget.
verdict_text: My verdict: Auth.js wins on Next.js integration (8/10 vs 7/10). The gap comes down to real-world App Router patterns  –  middleware, server components, edge runtime. Logto is absolutely usable, but Auth.js demands less friction. If budget is tight, Logto still delivers.

pros: Built-in management API and admin console
Support for social sign-in, MFA, and passwordless auth
User-friendly admin dashboard for non-technical team members
cons: Smaller community and fewer third-party integrations
Next.js SDK is less mature with fewer examples
Self-hosted version requires significant infrastructure setup

pros: Massive community with extensive documentation and examples
Framework-agnostic but excellent Next.js support via next-auth
Supports 80+ OAuth providers out of the box
Battle-tested in production across thousands of applications
cons: No built-in UI components - you build your own login pages
Configuration can become complex for advanced use cases
Database setup required for persistent sessions

faq.question: Should I choose Logto or Auth.js for my Next.js app?
faq.answer: It depends on your specific needs. Logto scores 7/10 for Next.js integration with a Medium learning curve. Auth.js scores 8/10 with a Medium learning curve. For most projects, we recommend Auth.js because of its stronger DX and better alignment with App Router patterns. However, if Yes, up to 5,000 MAU or Yes, fully open source is a deciding factor, the cheaper option may serve you well.

faq.question: Is Logto or Auth.js more affordable?
faq.answer: Logto starts at Free – $599/mo with a free tier of Yes, up to 5,000 MAU. Auth.js starts at Free (Open Source) with a free tier of Yes, fully open source. The total cost of ownership depends on your MAU (monthly active users). At lower volumes, the free tiers of both are sufficient. As you scale, Logto offers better value at scale. Factor in hidden costs like enterprise features, SSO, and database infrastructure when comparing.

faq.question: Is Logto or Auth.js easier to set up with Next.js?
faq.answer: Logto has a Medium learning curve with an estimated setup time of 20 minutes. Auth.js has a Medium learning curve with an estimated setup time of 20 minutes. Auth.js is faster to set up, but ease of integration also depends on your team's familiarity with the framework and whether you want pre-built UI components or prefer to build your own.

faq.question: Can I use Logto and Auth.js together?
faq.answer: Using both Logto and Auth.js in the same Next.js application is not recommended. They would create separate session management systems, conflicting middleware, and competing cookies. Choose one auth provider and stick with it. Migrating between auth providers later is possible but requires user data export and rebuilding authentication flows.

faq.question: Which has better developer experience: Logto or Auth.js?
faq.answer: Logto has a Medium learning curve, a Next.js integration score of 7/10, and a user rating of 4.1★. Auth.js has a Medium learning curve, 8/10, and 4.6★. On paper, Auth.js leads in DX. In practice, your specific requirements around pre-built components, customization, and team expertise matter just as much. Try both free tiers to see what clicks.
